title: Antisymmetric spontaneous resistivity anisotropy due to hard-axis collapse in polycrystalline Co thin films
authors: Y. Fernandes, J. Geshev, A. M. H. de Andrade, A. D. C. Viegas
abstract: We investigate magnetoresistance phenomena associated with the magnetization hard-axis collapse in polycrystalline Co thin films. Transport measurements reveal that, for specific orientations of the applied magnetic field, the system exhibits distinct remanent resistance levels in both the in-plane longitudinal and transverse voltage responses. In particular, the planar Hall resistance shows multiple stable and reproducible levels at room temperature, enabling the identification of at least three remanent states that can be distinguished and used for information storage. These resistance levels originate from non-uniform magnetic configurations stabilized after the application and removal of the external magnetic field in the hard-axis region. Since this phenomenon remains largely unexplored, we present an incipient study addressing its potential implications from an applied-physics perspective. The observation of such behavior in polycrystalline Co thin films grown on Si substrates suggests a simple and low-cost platform for spintronic memory and sensing devices based on the remanent planar Hall effect.
summary: This paper demonstrates that applying a magnetic field to polycrystalline Co thin films can create stable, multi-level magnetic states due to hard-axis collapse. These states are detectable via the planar Hall effect, offering a simple and low-cost platform for developing next-generation magnetic memory and sensors.
main_problem: Investigating the magnetoresistance phenomena associated with hard-axis collapse (HAC) in polycrystalline Co thin films and exploring its potential for multi-level magnetic memory and sensing.
main_result: The study identifies at least three stable, reproducible remanent resistance states in the planar Hall resistance, originating from non-uniform magnetic configurations, which could enable low-cost spintronic devices.
method: DC magnetron sputtering was used for film growth, followed by XRD for structural characterization, VSM for vector magnetometry, and four-point probe transport measurements at room temperature.
model_or_system: Polycrystalline Cobalt (Co) thin films (20 nm) grown on Si(100) substrates with various underlayers (Ta, TiO2, Al2O3) and induced in-plane uniaxial magnetic anisotropy.
key_observables: Longitudinal AMR (LAMR), Transverse AMR/Planar Hall Effect (TAMR/PHE), remanent magnetization (M_rem), and anisotropy field (Ha).
important_parameters: Anisotropy field (30-42 Oe), field angle (alpha), current density angle (phi_j), and remanent states (chi_1, chi_2, chi_3).
main_assumptions: The behavior is assumed to be robust across different thicknesses (5 to 150 nm) and structural variations.
figures_summary: XRD patterns showing crystallinity; angular variations of remanent magnetization; LAMR data at saturation and remanence; and the antisymmetric TAMR behavior near the hard axis.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the HAC phenomenon, details the experimental fabrication and structural characterization, presents transport and magnetometry results, discusses the physical mechanism of TAMR via spin-orbit coupling, and concludes with potential applications in spintronics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Room-temperature x-ray diffraction patterns in the vicinity of the Ta(200) peak and the Co fcc (111) and hcp (002) reflections for the three samples deposited under an in- plane magnetic field. An offset was applied to the B and C curves for clarity.

FIG. 2. Illustration of the studied systems. A Cartesian co- ordinate system is used, with the x-axis defined along the in- plane current density j, which is oriented at an angle ϕj with respect to the EA. The magnetization field M(x, y) forms an angle θM relative to j. The voltages Vxx and Vxy are measured along j and transverse to it in the xy plane, respectively.

FIG. 3. Angular variations of the magnetization and magne- toresistance for sample A. Panel (a) gives Mrem; in (b), the symbols present the magnetoresistance (LAMR and TAMR) at magnetic saturation while the lines correspond to a fitting curve, and panel (c) shows the corresponding data at rema- nence. For comparison, a curve proportional to dρxx/dα is also plotted in the vicinity of 270◦. All measurements were performed with ϕj ≈90◦.

FIG. 4. Angular Mrem and MR variations obtained for samples B and C for ϕj ≈90◦. The Mrem(ϕH)/MS curves are shown in panel (a); for clarity, an offset of 0.8 is applied. Panel (b) presents LAMR data obtained at Hsat and at remanence together with a fitting curve proportional to cos2(α −ϕj), while panels (c) and (d) show TAMR yielded at Hsat and at remanence for samples B and C, respectively, where fitting curves, ∼sin(2α −2ϕj), are plotted as well. Panel (e) illustrates a possible device concept for creating remanent states using simple quasi-coplanar perpendicular current channels.
